message:
chore(beads): file 4 multi-pass bug-hunting findings (cc_1)

Applied /multi-pass-bug-hunting to crates/frankenterm-core/. Worked
through the 4 passes the orchestrator named (panic/unwrap; race/
TOCTOU; error-path correctness; state-machine invariants). Filed 4
strong findings; the production codebase is otherwise exceptionally
well-defended on this axis (zero production .lock().unwrap() in
storage.rs's first 19499 lines, zero production unwraps in
recorder_storage.rs's first 1234 lines).

ft-xvrlp  [pass-1] replay_capture.rs:73, 88, 98, 104, 209 —
          Mutex::lock().unwrap() on the recorder hot path. Lock
          poisoning permanently bricks the recorder. Five sites,
          all on Recorder*Sink methods called from every captured
          event. Fix: poison-recovery via .unwrap_or_else(into_inner)
          or DashMap for lock-free reads.

ft-o2t7l  [pass-2] workflows/runner.rs:327, 336, 342 — RwLock
          unwraps on the workflow registry. Poisoned registry
          permanently bricks dispatch on every detection event.
          The architecture comment at line 463-465 even
          acknowledges the symmetric "lock that no workflow will
          ever release" pattern — the registry has the same
          surface, opposite direction. Fix: poison-recovery,
          optional ArcSwap for lock-free dispatch.

ft-oqfsx  [pass-3] storage.rs:3863 database_stats() — silent SQL
          errors via .ok() chain converts prepare/query/row
          failures to empty Vec. Used by ft doctor's diagnostic
          surface; corrupted DB reports green. Fix: per-stat error
          fields on DbStatsReport + telemetry counters + tracing
          warnings.

ft-qkd2f  [pass-4] workflows/runner.rs — pane lock has 14 manual
          release sites for 2 acquire sites. State-machine
          invariant "every acquire is followed by exactly one
          release on every path" is enforced by coding discipline,
          not the type system. Future error variants and
          panic-during-execution leak the lock permanently. Fix:
          PaneLockGuard scope guard with Drop-based release;
          replaces all 14 manual release calls with zero.

Each finding cites file:line, includes a concrete reproduction
sketch or attack scenario, names the skill pass it surfaced
under, and proposes a code-level fix that converts coding
discipline into type-system enforcement where possible.

Excluded as not-a-finding after investigation:
  - LazyLock regex .expect() (init-time programmer error only)
  - Drop impl on RecorderHandle that swallows flush() errors
    (documented best practice; Drop can't propagate)
  - block_on in storage.rs (covered by ft-3tvvt — modes-of-
    reasoning angle on async-vs-sync)
  - Tx phase transition (well-defended; can_transition_to gates
    transition_phase)
  - storage.rs first 19499 lines (zero production lock-unwraps)

Stopped at 4 findings within the 30-minute window. Skill says
"Stop after 5 findings or 30 min" — the production code is
genuinely well-defended on the multi-pass axes the orchestrator
scoped, so a 5th finding would require manufacturing one.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ft-atcr0","title":"backpressure::classify silently returns Green when config thresholds are NaN","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/backpressure.rs` at `BackpressureManager::classify` (lines 416-438):

```rust
if capture_saturated || write_saturated {
    BackpressureTier::Black
} else if cr >= self.config.red_capture || wr >= self.config.red_write {
    BackpressureTier::Red
} else if cr >= self.config.yellow_capture || wr >= self.config.yellow_write {
    BackpressureTier::Yellow
} else {
    BackpressureTier::Green
}
```

Any `>=` comparison with NaN is false. If the operator ships a BackpressureConfig where one or more thresholds is NaN (TOML supports `yellow_capture = nan`, `red_capture = nan`, etc.), every branch falls through to Green. The backpressure monitor reports 'healthy' regardless of actual queue depth — disabling every upgrade action (pane-pause, detection skipping, capacity shedding).

# Parallel to ft-761tz

This is the same shape as c5a02edb (ft-761tz, disk_pressure):
- disk_pressure `classify_tier` silently returned Green on NaN usage_fraction — fixed by fail-closed Black return at the top of classify_tier.
- EwmaEstimator / PidController silently propagated NaN through clamp — fixed by NaN guard at update.

backpressure.rs has not received the same hardening. Config thresholds are `f64` and deserialized via serde; TOML's `nan` literal slips through unchanged. No `validate()` method exists on BackpressureConfig.

# Fix

1. Add a `BackpressureConfig::validate()` method that rejects non-finite thresholds, negative values, and yellow ≥ red misordering. Emit a descriptive error.
2. Defense-in-depth inside `classify`: if ANY relevant config threshold or input ratio is non-finite, return `BackpressureTier::Black` to fail closed (same disposition as ft-761tz).
3. Regression tests:
   - config_validate_rejects_nan_yellow_capture
   - config_validate_rejects_red_below_yellow
   - classify_returns_black_on_nan_config_threshold (defense-in-depth path)

# Severity

MEDIUM — requires operator misconfiguration to trigger, but the failure mode is silent (no panic, no error) and the blast radius is 'backpressure is effectively disabled' which can let the watcher runaway into OOM under load. -{"id":"ft-b35tw","title":"[conformance] pattern-pack file format has no spec, no JSON-Schema, no fixture corpus from a reference loader","description":"## The gap

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs` parses YAML and JSON
pattern-pack manifests (built-in core packs, user packs, nested
`.ft/patterns/` discovery). The pack format is a public surface that
external rule authors and security auditors interact with, but there is no:

1. Authoritative spec document (no `docs/patterns-pack-format.md`,
   no entry in `docs/proposals/`).
2. JSON Schema or YAML schema published alongside the parser.
3. Reference fixture corpus pinning known-good packs across schema
   versions.
4. Spec-derived test matrix enumerating MUST/SHOULD clauses for the
   format.

## What exists today

- `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/proptest_patterns.rs` (52 KB) —
  excellent property tests for *engine behavior*.
- `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/proptest_patterns_metamorphic.rs` —
  metamorphic relations on triggering.
- `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/pattern_corpus.rs` — a runtime
  corpus, not a format-conformance corpus.
- `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/metamorphic_pattern_engine.rs`,
  `metamorphic_pattern_trigger_scan.rs` — behavior, not format.

None of these enforce format-version compatibility, validate that a
pack hand-authored against the format produces the documented Rust
struct, or guard against silent breaking changes when fields are
renamed.

## Why this catches real defects (skill criterion)

External rule authors today must read `patterns.rs` source to learn
the schema. When fields are renamed, deprecated, or moved between
nested manifests:

- Hand-authored packs in the wild break silently (parser drops
  unknown fields under default serde behavior).
- The `discover_packs_from_dir` symlink-escape fix (recent commit
  `6ab52765`) shows pack-loading is a security-sensitive surface;
  format drift compounds the risk surface.
- The `nested yaml/json pack manifests` discovery (`07e72a52`) is
  exactly the kind of feature where the format spec should pin
  the contract before more loaders depend on it.

A conformance harness with a reference corpus closes both holes:
external authors read the spec; CI catches drift between spec and
loader.

## Suggested implementation

1. **Format spec** (`docs/patterns-pack-format.md`):
   - Top-level structure (`name`, `version`, `format_version`,
     `rules`, etc.).
   - Each `Rule` field with type, presence (MUST/SHOULD/MAY), and
     example.
   - Versioning policy: how a `format_version` bump is signaled,
     what's backward-compatible, what's a breaking change.
   - Discovery rules (built-in vs `.ft/patterns/` vs user packs)
     and the symlink-escape sandbox guarantees.

2. **JSON Schema** (`docs/json-schema/ft-pattern-pack.json`):
   - Generate via `schemars` from the existing `Pack` / `Rule`
     structs (consistent with the strategy in `api_schema.rs`).
   - Add a row to `docs/json-schema/PROVENANCE.md` (per ft-d5268).

3. **Reference fixture corpus**
   (`crates/frankenterm-core/tests/fixtures/pattern_packs/`):
   - One subdirectory per supported `format_version`.
   - Each contains 5–10 hand-authored YAML+JSON pack examples that
     exercise every documented field.
   - Include intentionally-malformed packs (in
     `fixtures/pattern_packs/invalid/`) with the expected error
     class for each.

4. **Conformance test** (`tests/conformance_pattern_pack_format.rs`):
   - Loads every fixture pack, asserts the expected parse outcome.
   - Validates each accepted fixture against the JSON Schema.
   - Round-trips: parse → serialize → parse-equal-to-original (for
     formats that should round-trip).
   - Asserts every rule field documented in the spec is reachable
     from at least one fixture (coverage matrix in stdout).

## Out of scope

- Authoring new pattern rules (this is purely format infrastructure).
- The Aho-Corasick / regex engine layer (already covered by existing
  proptest suites).
- Runtime caching of compiled packs (recent perf work in
  `d05e5b7b`).

## Acceptance criteria

- `docs/patterns-pack-format.md` exists and is referenced from
  README's "Pattern Rules Tooling" section.
- A renamed field in `Rule` without a fixture+spec update fails the
  conformance test.
- A new fixture in `fixtures/pattern_packs/` whose schema validates
  but whose semantics violate a rule loader invariant produces a
  precise diagnostic, not silent acceptance.

## References

- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs` — the loader
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/pattern_trigger.rs` — the engine
- Recent format-touching commits: `6ab52765` (symlink escape),
  `07e72a52` (nested manifest discovery), `0239fbd6` (HIGH:
  canonicalize), `37384b01` (sandbox_resolve_dir)

 {"id":"ft-b4l62","title":"[MEDIUM] telemetry Histogram NaN-poisons total_sum/mean and emits nonsense quantiles on NaN q","description":"# Defect

## Concrete failure paths

File: `crates/frankenterm-core/src/telemetry.rs`, `struct Histogram`.

### 1. `Histogram::record(NaN)` permanently poisons the running mean

```rust
pub fn record(&mut self, value: f64) {
    self.total_count += 1;
    self.total_sum += value;    // NaN poisons total_sum permanently
    if value < self.min { self.min = value; }
    if value > self.max { self.max = value; }
    ...
}
```

Once any recorded value is NaN, `total_sum` is NaN forever (clean samples do nothing to restore it). Then `mean()` returns `Some(NaN)`:

```rust
pub fn mean(&self) -> Option<f64> {
    if self.total_count == 0 { return None; }
    Some(self.total_sum / self.total_count as f64)
}
```

A silently-wrong mean propagates into every `HistogramSummary`, JSON export, and dashboard forever.

### 2. `Histogram::quantile(NaN)` returns an arbitrary sample

```rust
let idx = ((sorted.len() as f64 - 1.0) * q.clamp(0.0, 1.0)) as usize;
```

`f64::clamp(NaN, 0.0, 1.0)` returns NaN (documented gotcha). `(NaN) as usize` is 0 in Rust. So `quantile(NaN)` returns `Some(sorted[0])` — an arbitrary 'smallest' sample rather than `None`. Combined with a NaN sample in the retained window, sort order is non-deterministic, and the output is effectively undefined.

### 3. Classify: silent wrong-answer

Not a panic, not data loss — operators looking at a dashboard see a mean that says NaN (or a quantile that says zero) and assume the subsystem is healthy.

## Reachability

`Histogram::record` is called from `MetricRegistry::record_histogram` which takes an f64. Callers that pass `dt.as_secs_f64()` are safe in practice, but any caller doing arithmetic (rate = delta_samples / delta_time with delta_time = 0.0) can produce NaN. Same class as ft-761tz (disk_pressure clamp-doesn't-sanitise-NaN) and ft-atcr0 (backpressure config validate).

## Fix

1. `Histogram::record`: if `value.is_nan()`, return early (no counter bump, no sum update, no sample push). This matches ft-761tz's fail-closed precedent.
2. `Histogram::quantile`: if `q.is_nan()`, return `None` explicitly — don't let the `clamp` + `as usize` cast silently produce index 0.
3. Regression tests covering both defenses.

## Scope

Single file (`telemetry.rs`). No public API change (NaN input is still accepted, just no longer poisonous).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:39:42.897340Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:46:08.381499Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:46:08.380804Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-b4mif","title":"[review][MEDIUM] README Quickstart claims ft --version emits rustc/target/features but it only emits semver+git","description":"README Quickstart section (landed in bcc668a0, Apr 23 13:54) claims:

  ft --version                 # semver + git commit + rustc + target + features

The clap version wiring at crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:67-72 binds version = CLAP_VERSION.as_str() where CLAP_VERSION = build_meta::short_version() (lines 39-41). short_version() returns only:

  format!("{} ({}{})", frankenterm_core::VERSION, GIT_HASH, GIT_DIRTY)

i.e. semver + git commit + optional dirty marker. The rustc/target/features block lives in build_meta::verbose_version() (main.rs:43-61) and is ONLY reachable via the separate ft version --full subcommand (main.rs:16718-16724), NOT ft --version.

Without #[command(long_version = ...)] in the clap derive, ft --version and ft -V both surface short_version() only. A first-touch operator following the Quickstart will copy the command, not see what the docs promised, and have to chase where rustc/target/features actually show up.

Fix options:
1. Update README quickstart to:
     ft --version                 # semver + git commit (+ -dirty marker)
     ft version --full            # semver + git commit + built + rustc + target + features
2. OR add long_version = build_meta::verbose_version() to the Cli derive at crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:72 so ft --version actually emits the full block (ft -V stays short).

Option 2 is a stronger fix because it makes the README literally true, but the operator expectation today is 'short for --version, full for version --full', and changing that flips the UX for every script that pipes ft --version into grep. Option 1 (doc fix) is safer.

Severity: MEDIUM — no functional bug, but first-touch operator expectation drift.  {"id":"ft-nvr6a","title":"[golden-artifacts] search_explain: 71KB module, zero golden coverage for ExplainPlan JSON or render_explain_plain output","description":"## The gap

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/search_explain.rs` (71 KB) is the
user-facing search debugging surface — what `ft search --explain`,
`ft robot search-explain`, and the MCP `wa.search.explain` tool emit.

It has structured types:

- `SearchExplainResult` (line 34)
- `SearchExplainEvidence` (line 25)
- `SearchExplainReason` (line 10)
- `SearchExplainContext` (line 53)
- `PaneExplainInfo` (line 76)
- `PaneIndexingInfo` (line 86)
- `GapInfo` (line 97)

Two formatters:

- `pub fn explain_search(ctx: &SearchExplainContext) -> SearchExplainResult` (line 254)
- `pub fn render_explain_plain(result: &SearchExplainResult) -> String` (line 627)

Tests today: `tests/search/proptest_search_explain.rs` (39 KB of
property tests). Property tests check shape invariants — they do NOT
pin exact output. `grep -lE "search_explain.*golden\|explain_plan_golden"`
returns zero hits across `tests/`.

## Why this catches real defects (skill criterion)

The plain-text format is what an operator reads when debugging a
"search returns no hits" incident. A regression in:
- The `Reason` ordering (e.g., "no matching pane" surfaces before
  "FTS index out of date")
- The evidence-line phrasing
- The truncation/elision of long pane lists
- The ranking score formatting

…breaks every operator's mental model. Property tests don't catch
these because the *shape* is preserved. Goldens do.

The JSON form is the same risk for downstream consumers (the
`ft robot search-explain` MCP tool feeds AI agents — if the JSON
shape shifts, agent prompts break silently).

## Suggested implementation

 {"id":"ft-nyvyl","title":"[HIGH] mux TLS listener accept() has no handshake timeout → slow-loris wedges per-domain accept loop","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-24T18:39:36.728112Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-24T19:25:59.293234Z","closed_at":"2026-04-24T19:25:59.292972Z","close_reason":"Shipped in ed413bd2: bound mux TLS handshakes to 5s","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["review","runtime","security"],"comments":[{"id":3640,"issue_id":"ft-nyvyl","author":"jemanuel","text":"# Gap

crates/frankenterm-mux-server/src/ossl.rs:80-109:

    match stream {
        Ok(stream) => {
            stream.set_nodelay(true).ok();
            let acceptor = self.acceptor.clone();
            let dispatch_config = self.dispatch_config;
            match acceptor.accept(stream) {
                Ok(stream) => { ... spawn async dispatch ... }
                Err(e) => log::error!("failed TlsAcceptor: {}", e),
            }
        }
        ...
    }

Two problems compound:

1. `stream` is a `std::net::TcpStream` and nothing calls
   `set_read_timeout()` / `set_write_timeout()` on it before
   `acceptor.accept(stream)`. The OpenSSL handshake reads/writes on the
   raw blocking socket.
2. `acceptor.accept(stream)` is executed SYNCHRONOUSLY on the accept
   thread, not the async runtime. A peer that opens a TCP socket and
   refuses to advance the TLS handshake (or drip-feeds it a byte at a
   time) parks this thread indefinitely.

Because the accept loop iterates after accept() returns, a single slow /
malicious peer halts every subsequent legitimate connection for this
TLS domain.

# Comparison with client side

frankenterm/client/src/client.rs:1266-1267 does call `set_read_timeout`
and `set_write_timeout` on the client-side TCP stream before initiating
the handshake — so the operator-side connection is protected, but the
server-side isn't.

# Why this matters

This is a zero-credential DoS against the mux-server TLS domain. Any
peer that can reach the TLS port (even with an invalid cert — note
that peer-cert verification only runs AFTER accept() at line 88) can
wedge the listener.

The `set_read_timeout` landing was partial: ft-kovt3 wired it for the
client; this bead extends it to the mux-server accept path, which is
the remaining half.

# Shape of fix (≤30 LOC)

Before `acceptor.accept(stream)` in ossl.rs, set a reasonable handshake
deadline on the raw TCP stream, then clear it once the handshake
completes and before wrapping in AsyncSslStream:

    stream.set_read_timeout(Some(HANDSHAKE_TIMEOUT))?;
    stream.set_write_timeout(Some(HANDSHAKE_TIMEOUT))?;
    let stream = match acceptor.accept(stream) {
        Ok(s) => s,
        Err(e) => { log::error!("failed TlsAcceptor: {}", e); continue; }
    };
    stream.get_ref().set_read_timeout(None).ok();
    stream.get_ref().set_write_timeout(None).ok();

A constant like `const HANDSHAKE_TIMEOUT: Duration = Duration::from_secs(10);`
is conservative and aligned with OpenSSL session ticket recommendations.

The `continue` (rather than `break`) on accept failure is also worth a
second look while the file is open — the current `break` at line 90 for
verify_peer_cert errors exits the whole accept loop, which is a
separate DoS lever.

# Out of scope

The select-arm-drop / registration concern raised in the audit is
working as designed (the stream outlives the dropped future; the
IoRegistration lives on the stream, not on the dropped future). No bead.

The capture→relay→persistence shutdown ordering in runtime.rs is also
correct: capture.await completes before relay.await, and relay closes
the ring BEFORE persistence.await is entered (runtime.rs:4124-4141).
No bead.

The prune_segments_sync / append_segment_sync race is bounded by
SQLite's single-writer rule on the DB file; the fts_pane_progress
rewind runs inside the same implicit transaction as the DELETE
(storage.rs:14157-14196). No bead.

The ft-nrbrp Cancelled-collapse pattern is already handled in the
relay loop via explicit RecvEvent::{Item, Closed, Cancelled} match
(runtime.rs:2986-3010). No follow-up bead.","created_at":"2026-04-24T18:40:04Z"}]}
 {"id":"ft-o2mtn","title":"[profiling] build_semantic_chunks: semantic-search index ingest hot path; zero benches","description":"## Hypothesis

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/search/chunking.rs:242`
(`build_semantic_chunks`) is the semantic-search ingest hot path.
Every pane delta that flows into the semantic index runs through
this function; the chunker decides where to split text into
semantic units, which determines both the embedding cost
(downstream — fastembed / safetensors) and the recall quality.

`grep -lE "Chunking|chunk_text|build_semantic_chunks"
benches/*.rs` returns nothing. `semantic_anomaly_bench.rs` and
`semantic_compression.rs` cover different surfaces.

## Why a profile + bench would deliver real perf gain

Semantic search behind the `--features semantic-search` flag is a
tier-1 user-visible feature (per README's "Lexical + Hybrid
Search" feature line). The chunker decides:

- How many embedding calls per ingested document — directly
  drives semantic-search latency budget.
- How much text per chunk — determines the embedding model's
  context utilization (under-fill = wasted compute, over-fill =
  truncation).
- Allocation pattern — string slicing vs new String allocation
  per chunk dominates cost on small chunks.

A tighter chunker is a multiplier on every downstream
(embedding, indexing, hybrid-fusion) hop. Without a benchmark we
can't tell whether the current `ChunkPolicyConfig` defaults are
sensible or pathological.

## Suggested instrumentation + bench

1. **Bench**: `crates/frankenterm-core/benches/semantic_chunking.rs`,
   criterion harness with workloads from realistic pane corpora:
   - `tests/corpus/codex/*.txt` (long agent output)
   - `tests/corpus/claude_code/*.txt`
   - `tests/corpus/wezterm/*.txt`
   - synthetic worst-case: 1MB of mixed alt-screen + control
     sequences (CSI bombs)
   For each: vary chunk-size policy
   `(small | default | large)` and measure ms/MB, chunks/MB,
   alloc count/MB.

2. **Allocation attribution**: dhat run — track per-chunk allocs.
   Likely candidates: `String::from(&str)`, `Vec<SemanticChunk>`
   growth, regex string slice escapes.

3. **Scaling law**: chunk count scales sublinearly with input?
   If linear, no headroom; if sublinear, optimization opportunity.

4. **Differential** with a hand-coded "chunker" that just splits
   on `\n` — establishes a floor for "what's the cost of NOT
   doing semantic chunking?"

## Hypothesis ledger entries

```
default-chunk-size-too-small : pending — measure embedding hops
                                per MB at default vs large
chunker-allocs-per-chunk     : pending — dhat shows heap allocs
                                proportional to chunks/MB
ansi-stripping-dominates     : pending — pane corpora include
                                control sequences; flame shows
                                strip cost > split cost
worst-case-pinning           : pending — alt-screen-heavy input
                                has p99 > 10× p50
```

## Out of scope

- The downstream embedding (fastembed / safetensors) cost — that
  needs its own bead and `LLM-AI-PROFILING.md` framing.
- The `SemanticChunk` serde shape (covered by
  `proptest_chunking.rs`).

## Acceptance criteria

- `benches/semantic_chunking.rs` exists, registered in Cargo.toml
  under the `semantic-search` feature.
- Three real-corpus + one synthetic workload, measured at
  `(small/default/large)` policy combinations.
- Profile run produces a hotspot table showing the top-3 cost
  centers (likely: regex.match, String allocs, Vec growth).
- Hypothesis ledger entries marked with evidence paths.

## References

- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/search/chunking.rs:242`
- `tests/corpus/{codex,claude_code,wezterm}/` for realistic input
- README §Lexical + Hybrid Search
- Skill: profiling-software-performance — operator card "Spike"
  for synthetic worst-case tail measurement

+{"id":"ft-o2t7l","title":"[multi-pass][pass-2] workflows/runner.rs:327,336,342: RwLock unwraps on workflow registry brick all workflow lookups on poison","description":"## Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/workflows/runner.rs:272`:

```rust
pub struct WorkflowRunner {
    workflows: std::sync::RwLock<Vec<Arc<dyn Workflow>>>,
    ...
}
```

with three production call sites:

```rust
// line 327 — register_workflow
let mut workflows = self.workflows.write().unwrap();

// line 336 — find_matching_workflow
let workflows = self.workflows.read().unwrap();

// line 342 — find_workflow_by_name
let workflows = self.workflows.read().unwrap();
```

The `WorkflowRunner` is shared (typically `Arc<WorkflowRunner>`) across
all detection events for the lifetime of the watch daemon. If ANY
panic happens inside `register_workflow` while holding the `write`
lock — e.g., a panic inside `Vec::push` under memory pressure, or any
future trait-object construction that panics — the RwLock is
poisoned and every subsequent `find_matching_workflow` /
`find_workflow_by_name` call panics.

Per the architecture comment at runner.rs:243, every detection event
flows through this lookup. A poisoned registry means **the entire
event-driven workflow surface is dead until process restart** — and
the daemon doesn't know it's dead because the panic occurs inside
the dispatcher, not at registration time.

## Why this is a real defect

Concrete failure scenario:
1. Daemon starts, registers 10 workflows successfully.
2. Operator hot-reloads workflows via a (future or live) reload
   path that calls `register_workflow` and panics mid-push.
3. RwLock is poisoned.
4. Every subsequent detection event panics in `handle_detection`.
5. The watcher's outer panic handler catches the panic, logs it,
   and the workflow runner returns Error envelopes for every
   detection — silently, at runtime, invisible until an operator
   notices NO workflows are firing.

Per skill Pass 2 (race conditions / TOCTOU / async ordering), this
is the classic "lock unwrap creates a permanent fail-state" pattern.
Per the security-audit Axiom "every fail-open is a DoS pivot,"
poisoned-lock is the symmetric "every fail-closed is a permanent
DoS surface."

## Suggested fix

Replace each `.unwrap()` with poison recovery:

```rust
pub fn register_workflow(&self, workflow: Arc<dyn Workflow>) {
    let mut workflows = self.workflows
        .write()
        .unwrap_or_else(|poisoned| poisoned.into_inner());
    workflows.push(workflow);
}

pub fn find_matching_workflow(
    &self,
    detection: &crate::patterns::Detection,
) -> Option<Arc<dyn Workflow>> {
    let workflows = self.workflows
        .read()
        .unwrap_or_else(|poisoned| poisoned.into_inner());
    workflows.iter().find(|w| w.handles(detection)).cloned()
}
```

The data is `Vec<Arc<dyn Workflow>>` — recovery from poison is
trivially safe. Workflows are append-only; even if a registration
panicked mid-push, the worst outcome is "the panicking
registration didn't add the workflow," which is the correct
recovery state.

Bonus: consider `arc_swap::ArcSwap<Vec<Arc<dyn Workflow>>>` for
lock-free reads on the hot dispatch path (`find_matching_workflow`
runs once per detection event — easily 1000s/sec at fleet scale).

## Acceptance criteria

- All three sites at `workflows/runner.rs:327, 336, 342` use the
  poison-recovery pattern.
- Lines 1986, 2204 (test-side `RwLock::new(vec![...])`) are NOT
  affected (test code is allowed to unwrap).
- A regression test poisons the lock by panicking inside
  `register_workflow` and asserts subsequent
  `find_matching_workflow` calls succeed (return `None`, not
  panic).
- Bonus: perf bench compares `RwLock::read` vs `arc_swap::load`
  on the dispatch path; if dispatch CPU is non-trivial, swap to
  `ArcSwap`.

## References

- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/workflows/runner.rs:272, 327, 336, 342`
- README §Architecture (workflow runner is dispatch-once-per-detection)
- Skill: multi-pass-bug-hunting Pass 2 ("permanent fail-state from
  lock unwrap")
- ft-ao9k9 (sibling: external-signal wait wiring; related runner
  hardening)

 {"id":"ft-o657v","title":"conformance_mcp_manifest: pin tool input_schema additionalProperties:false invariant","description":"# Gap

`crates/frankenterm-core/tests/conformance_mcp_manifest.rs` rejects unknown top-level fields (via `#[serde(deny_unknown_fields)]`), checks uniqueness of tool/resource/prompt names, and validates minimal/optional field presence. But it does NOT pin a security-relevant invariant that every shipped MCP tool CURRENTLY satisfies: **every tool's `input_schema` has `additionalProperties: false`**.

Verified by:

    $ rg -c 'additionalProperties' crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs
    29
    $ rg -c 'input_schema:' crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs
    29

All 29 input_schemas currently set it. Without a test pinning this, a future tool author could add a new tool without the clause and the server would silently accept extra fields from untrusted MCP clients — side-channel surface for policy bypass, audit log pollution, or ambiguous-field attacks on tools that later try to evolve their schema.

# Fix

Add `every_tool_input_schema_denies_additional_properties` in conformance_mcp_manifest.rs that iterates `server.tools()` from `build_server_with_db(&Config::default(), Some(...))` and asserts each `tool.input_schema` is a JSON object with `"additionalProperties": false` at the top level. If a tool's schema is NOT type=object (e.g. `{}`, or a primitive), flag that too — all MCP tools in this server should accept structured object arguments.

# Non-fix

Middleware-injected `format` property (from augment_tool_schema_with_format in mcp_middleware.rs) is a known accepted exception because it's stripped from arguments before the inner call. The test should assert additionalProperties:false; the format injection happens to the properties map, not to additionalProperties, so the test passes cleanly.

# Severity

MEDIUM — no live defect; every current tool sets the bit. But regression risk is real as new tools land, and a silently-missing additionalProperties:false turns into a real policy bypass if the tool ever evolves its allowed field set.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-22T12:03:57.448008Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T12:06:52.683351Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T12:06:52.683105Z","close_reason":"Landed as part of f73e139a (absorbed commit). every_tool_input_schema_denies_additional_properties iterates server.tools() from build_server_with_db + asserts each input_schema is type=object with additionalProperties literal false — pins the 29/29 invariant so future tools without the clause break CI instead of silently widening the accepted field set.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

## Background
Selection rendering and overlay layers are blend-heavy — easy to
break in renderer changes that touch alpha or layering order.

## Why this matters
- Selection is the most-frequent user interaction with cursor-on-text.

  overlays all use the same blending substrate.

2. **selection-word/**: word-mode selection (double-click style).
3. **selection-line/**: line-mode selection (triple-click).

5. **overlay-search-hits/**: a search query with multiple highlighted
   hits in different colors (e.g., current hit + other matches).

## Out of scope
- Animated selection extension (drag preview) — out of scope for
  static fixtures.

 {"id":"ft-ombfl.9","title":"[ft-ombfl][gpu-fixture] Multi-pane layout fixture: split divider geometry + focused/unfocused borders + 3+ panes","description":"# GPU fixture — multi-pane layout

## Background
ft-1memj.28 closed the divider-geometry test (bf9db5d5) — that test
pinned a single split's geometry. This fixture goes broader:
multi-pane layouts (2/3/4+ panes), focused vs unfocused border
treatment, and the cell-padding around dividers.

## Why this matters
- frankenterm is a swarm-native terminal — multi-pane layouts are
  the dominant use case (the 2026-04-27 swarm itself ran with 7
  panes). Regressions in multi-pane render are core breakage.
- Divider rendering is shared across split, tab, and floating
  surfaces — a fix in one can break the others.

## Scope
Author 5 fixtures under tests/golden/gpu/:
1. **multipane-2split-h/**: horizontal split, 2 panes side-by-side,
   left focused.
2. **multipane-2split-v/**: vertical split, 2 panes stacked, top
   focused.
3. **multipane-grid-4/**: 2x2 grid of panes, top-left focused.
4. **multipane-deep-nested/**: 5-pane layout with mixed splits
   (test divider corners and T-joins).
5. **multipane-floating-overlay/**: a floating pane on top of a
   normal split layout.

All fixtures: cursor blink off, deterministic content per pane.

## Out of scope
- Resize animation (static frames only).
- Tab bar rendering (separate fixture if needed).

## Approach
1. Author input.json for each, with explicit pane tree + focus state.
2. meta.json specifies viewport size, dpi, divider color.
3. Generate goldens.
4. Manual inspection: divider lines connect at corners, focused
   border is visibly distinct, padding is consistent.
5. Commit.

## Acceptance criteria
- All 5 fixtures committed.
- Tests pass.
- Divider corners are pixel-aligned (T-joins, +-joins, L-joins).
- Focused vs unfocused border is visibly distinct in goldens.
- Floating overlay correctly shadows underlying panes if shadow
  is enabled.

## Testing
- Fixture self-test.
- Mutation: change focus from one pane to another; confirm fixture
  fails with diff showing border swap.
- Mutation: shift one pane by 1 cell; confirm divider misalignment
  catches.
- Logging: per-fixture render_ms; multi-pane should scale roughly
  linearly with pane count (sanity check; not a perf gate).

## Risks / considerations
- Float-overlay shadow rendering may include per-frame randomness
  (e.g., dithering). Pin a deterministic shadow algorithm or
  disable shadows in meta.json.
- Deep-nested layout's divider intersections are the trickiest;
  triple-check corner pixel by manual inspection before committing.

+{"id":"ft-oqfsx","title":"[multi-pass][pass-3] storage.rs:3863 database_stats(): silent SQL errors via .ok() chain — ft doctor reports green on corrupt DB","description":"## Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs:3863`:

```rust
pub fn database_stats(db_path: &Path, retention_days: u32) -> DbStatsReport {
    ...
    let pane_stats = {
        let mut stmt = conn
            .prepare(\"SELECT ... FROM output_segments s ...\")
            .ok();                          // ← swallows prepare error
        match stmt.as_mut() {
            Some(s) => s
                .query_map([], |row| { ... })
                .ok()                       // ← swallows query error
                .map(|rows| rows.filter_map(|r| r.ok()).collect())   // ← swallows row error
                .unwrap_or_default(),       // ← swallows everything else
            None => vec![],                 // ← prepare-failed → empty Vec
        }
    };
    // identical pattern at 3944-3963 for event_type stats
    ...
}
```

The function returns `DbStatsReport` (NOT `Result<DbStatsReport>`).
ALL SQL errors — prepare failures, query failures, row deserialize
failures — are silently converted to empty `Vec<PaneStats>` /
`Vec<EventTypeStats>`. The caller has no way to distinguish
"DB has zero panes" from "DB schema is corrupt and prepare failed."

Per the function's apparent role (called by `ft doctor` JSON output
and the diagnostic surface), this is a real observability bug:

- **Operator runs `ft doctor --json` after a partial schema
  migration that left a table missing.**
- **The `prepare(\"SELECT ... FROM output_segments\")` returns Err
  because the table doesn't exist.**
- `.ok()` converts to `None`.
- `match stmt.as_mut() { ... None => vec![] }`.
- Doctor reports `pane_stats: []` (zero panes).
- Operator reads "looks fine — no panes captured yet."
- Real diagnosis: the recorder has been silently failing for hours
  because the `output_segments` table is gone.

## Why this is a real defect

This violates the skill's Pass 3 contract (error-path correctness):
**SQL errors are real signals**. The current code converts them to
"no data" — semantically the same as a successful empty query.

The function's `unwrap_or(0)` pattern at line 3902 (the `count = ...
unwrap_or(0)` for table sizing) has the same issue: a table-missing
error gets normalized to zero.

This compounds the security audit's ft-3se13 finding (decision log
unredacted) — the surfaces operators rely on to verify ft is
healthy give false-green.

## Suggested fix

Three options (preferring B):

A. **Change return type to `Result<DbStatsReport>`.** Most invasive,
   forces every caller to handle. Probably overkill for the doctor
   surface where graceful degradation is wanted.

B. **Introduce per-stat error fields**:

   ```rust
   pub struct DbStatsReport {
       pub pane_stats: Vec<PaneStats>,
       pub pane_stats_error: Option<String>,    // ← NEW
       pub event_types: Vec<EventTypeStats>,
       pub event_types_error: Option<String>,   // ← NEW
       ...
   }
   ```

   Each `.ok()` site captures the error message into the corresponding
   `_error` field. `ft doctor` JSON output exposes these; operators
   can grep for non-null errors.

C. **Log + telemetry counter on every swallowed error**:

   ```rust
   .map_err(|e| {
       tracing::warn!(error = %e, \"database_stats: prepare failed\");
       crate::telemetry::counter!(\"database_stats.error\").increment(1);
       e
   })
   .ok()
   ```

Option B + C combined is best: structured field + observability counter.

## Acceptance criteria

- `DbStatsReport` exposes a per-stat error field (or equivalent)
  for every `.ok()` swallow site.
- `ft doctor --json` output includes the error fields when set.
- A regression test simulates a missing-table state and asserts the
  error is surfaced (not silently zero).
- Telemetry counter increments on every swallowed error.

## References

- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs:3863-3963` (database_stats
  function body with the .ok() chain)
- README §Diagnostics (ft doctor is the marketed surface)
- ft-40k1o (sibling: ft doctor golden coverage gap — this defect is
  exactly what the goldens would catch if they existed)
- Skill: multi-pass-bug-hunting Pass 3 ("error-path correctness")

+{"id":"ft-qkd2f","title":"[multi-pass][pass-4] workflows/runner.rs: pane lock has 14 manual release sites; lock-leak invariant depends on every error path remembering","description":"## Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/workflows/runner.rs` has TWO acquire
sites:

```
367:  let lock_result = match self.lock_manager.try_acquire_with_limit(...
494:  let lock_result = match self.lock_manager.try_acquire_with_limit(...
```

…and FOURTEEN release sites scattered throughout the execution
path:

```
449   self.lock_manager.release(pane_id, &execution_id);
567   self.lock_manager.release(pane_id, &execution_id);
741   self.lock_manager.release(pane_id, execution_id);
809   self.lock_manager.release(pane_id, execution_id);
1057  self.lock_manager.release(pane_id, execution_id);
1113  self.lock_manager.release(pane_id, execution_id);
1166  self.lock_manager.release(pane_id, execution_id);
1225  self.lock_manager.release(pane_id, execution_id);
1282  self.lock_manager.release(pane_id, execution_id);
1324  self.lock_manager.release(pane_id, execution_id);
1371  self.lock_manager.release(pane_id, execution_id);
1418  self.lock_manager.release(pane_id, execution_id);
1531  self.lock_manager.release(pane_id, execution_id);
…
```

The state-machine invariant "every acquire is followed by exactly
one release on every path" is enforced by manual coding discipline
across 14 sites. **Any future refactor that adds a new early-
return path without a release is a permanent pane lock leak**:

- The pane stays locked.
- No subsequent workflow can drive it.
- The locked pane is a permanent DoS surface for that pane_id
  across the daemon's lifetime.
- The leak is invisible until an operator notices "workflow X
  never fires on pane Y anymore."

This is a Pass 4 finding per the skill: **state-machine invariant
that depends on coding discipline, not type-system enforcement**.

## Why this is a real defect

Three concrete drift hazards:

1. **Future error variants.** A new `WorkflowExecutionResult`
   variant added without a paired `release` call leaks the lock
   forever. The compiler doesn't enforce the pairing.

2. **Refactoring confusion.** Splitting any of the 14 release-
   site branches across two functions risks dropping the release
   in the split.

3. **Panic during execute_steps.** Today the panic-handler at
   the watch-daemon level catches panics, but the panic exits
   the function via unwinding — bypassing the manual `release`
   call. A panicking workflow leaks its pane lock.

## Suggested fix

Replace the manual release pattern with a **scope guard**:

```rust
struct PaneLockGuard<'a> {
    lock_manager: &'a PaneWorkflowLockManager,
    pane_id: u64,
    execution_id: String,
    released: bool,
}

impl<'a> PaneLockGuard<'a> {
    fn new(lock_manager: &'a PaneWorkflowLockManager, pane_id: u64, execution_id: String) -> Self {
        Self { lock_manager, pane_id, execution_id, released: false }
    }
    /// Explicit early release (e.g., before handing off to a sub-task).
    fn release(mut self) {
        self.lock_manager.release(self.pane_id, &self.execution_id);
        self.released = true;
    }
}

impl<'a> Drop for PaneLockGuard<'a> {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        if !self.released {
            self.lock_manager.release(self.pane_id, &self.execution_id);
        }
    }
}
```

…then every acquire site becomes:

```rust
let lock = match self.lock_manager.try_acquire_with_limit(...) {
    Ok(_) => PaneLockGuard::new(&self.lock_manager, pane_id, execution_id.clone()),
    Err(e) => return WorkflowStartResult::PaneLocked { ... },
};
// ...lock auto-released on every error path AND on panic-unwind.
```

The 14 manual `release` sites become zero. Refactoring becomes
safe. Panic-during-execution can no longer leak. **The state-
machine invariant is now enforced by the type system + Drop, not
coding discipline.**

## Acceptance criteria

- `PaneLockGuard` type exists with a `release(self)` consuming
  method + `Drop` fallback.
- All 14 manual `lock_manager.release(...)` calls in
  `workflows/runner.rs` are removed.
- A regression test triggers a panic inside `execute_steps` and
  asserts the pane lock is released (no leak).
- Type-system check: removing a `release()` call shouldn't break
  release semantics (because it's now Drop-enforced).

## Out of scope

- The `PaneWorkflowLockManager` internals (existing fairness +
  cycle detection logic).
- Replacing the lock manager itself (separate concern).

## References

- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/workflows/runner.rs:367, 494`
  (acquire sites)
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/workflows/runner.rs:449, 567,
  741, 809, 1057, 1113, 1166, 1225, 1282, 1324, 1371, 1418,
  1531, ...` (14+ release sites)

  enforcement: type system over coding discipline")

 {"id":"ft-xv561","title":"[HIGH] pattern-engine regex construction has no backtrack limit — workspace-local ReDoS via user packs","description":"Found during /security-audit-for-saas pass #2 (pane 5, 2026-04-21).

## Summary
The pattern engine compiles user-supplied regex patterns via
`fancy_regex::Regex::new(raw)` with NO backtrack limit or match
timeout. An attacker who can drop a YAML/JSON pattern pack into
`~/.config/wa/patterns/` or a repo's `.ft/patterns/` directory can
ship a catastrophic-backtracking regex that hangs `ft watch` the
first time matching pane output triggers the pattern's anchor.

## Evidence
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs:13
  `use fancy_regex::Regex;`
- patterns.rs:512 (RuleDef::validate_regex)
  `Regex::new(regex).map_err(...)` — no `.backtrack_limit(...)`.
- patterns.rs:780 (build_engine_index)
  `let regex = Regex::new(raw).map_err(...)` — same.
- patterns.rs:920 (load_packs_from_config) + :935
  (load_pack_from_id) — pack files are loaded from
  `config.resolved_user_packs_dir()` (default
  `~/.config/wa/patterns/`) AND from `.ft/patterns/` inside the
  resolved workspace root. The latter is an attacker-controlled
  directory when a repo is cloned from an untrusted source.
- mcp.rs:2010 (pattern-engine matching hot path)
  invokes `regex.captures_iter(text)` on every segment for every
  rule whose anchor matched. A single-threaded ReDoS blocks the
  matching worker; fancy_regex's default backtracking engine has
  no internal budget.

## Attack vector
1. attacker commits `.ft/patterns/evil.json` with a rule like
   ```json
   {
     "id": "evil.dos",
     "agent_type": "wezterm",
     "event_type": "noop",
     "anchors": ["a"],
     "regex": "^(a+)+$"
   }
   ```
2. user clones repo and runs `ft watch`.
3. the first pane output containing `"aaa...aaab"` (any near-match
   of the anchor) triggers captures_iter, which enters catastrophic
   backtracking. The pattern worker hangs indefinitely, wedging the
   detection pipeline.

No code execution — DoS only. But `ft watch` is the primary
operator surface; wedging the detection loop silently destroys
the "intelligent detection" claim the README makes.

## Fix direction
Two layers:

1. Compilation-time gate (quick):
   - Replace `Regex::new(raw)` with `fancy_regex::RegexBuilder::new(raw).backtrack_limit(1_000_000).build()`
     at patterns.rs:512 and :780. 1M steps is plenty for any
     legitimate rule and caps worst-case match cost.
   - Propagate the resulting `fancy_regex::Error::RuntimeError` from
     `captures_iter` as a `Detection` of kind `RegexRuntimeError`
     instead of hanging.

2. Match-time budget (robust):
   - Wrap each `regex.captures_iter(text)` call in a time-bounded
     harness (e.g., spawn a short-lived asupersync task with a
     100ms timeout). On timeout, quarantine the offending rule and
     surface a "rule disabled due to catastrophic match cost"
     telemetry event so operators can triage.

## Severity rationale
- HIGH (P1): attacker-controlled repo → DoS on user's machine.
- Not CRITICAL: local exploitation only (repo-clone vector), no
  network reach, no data exfil, no code execution.

## Out of scope for this bead
- RE2/Hyperscan migration (much larger rework).
- Per-rule memory budget (separate concern).

## Related
- ft-security-mcp-path-traversal (30988e53) — same audit pass
  already fixed the MCP arbitrary-file-read vector.

Filed per /security-audit-for-saas pass #2; recommend shipping the
compilation-time gate within one follow-up session.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T04:04:22.269417Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T04:14:08.976852Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T04:14:08.976108Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 070117a0: fix(patterns): cap regex backtracking at 10M steps to close ft-xv561 pattern-engine ReDoS on attacker-controlled pattern packs","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","patterns","redos","security"]}
+{"id":"ft-xvrlp","title":"[multi-pass][pass-1] replay_capture.rs:209: Mutex::lock().unwrap() on recorder hot path bricks recorder permanently on poison","description":"## Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/replay_capture.rs:209`:

```rust
fn next_pane_seq(&self, pane_id: u64) -> u64 {
    let mut map = self.pane_sequences.lock().unwrap();
    let counter = map.entry(pane_id).or_insert_with(|| AtomicU64::new(0));
    counter.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed)
}
```

`next_pane_seq` is called from `capture_egress` and `capture_ingress`
on the recorder's hot path — every captured event allocates a
sequence number through this Mutex. If ANY thread panics while
holding `pane_sequences.lock()` (e.g., during `or_insert_with` if
allocation fails under memory pressure, or any future code change
that introduces a panic-during-mutation), the lock becomes poisoned
and every subsequent `.lock().unwrap()` call panics — effectively
disabling the recorder for the lifetime of the process.

Lines 73, 88, 98, 104 in the same file have the same pattern on
`self.events.lock().unwrap()`, the in-memory event vector that
backs `RecorderEventSink::Memory`. Same risk.

## Why this is a real defect

Long-running daemon processes (the `ft watch` watcher is exactly
this — runs for hours/days) cannot afford permanent recorder loss
from a transient panic. The README markets the recorder as load-
bearing for "Replay & Forensics" — silently disabled-by-poison
recorder makes post-incident analysis fail with no diagnostic.

A panic during `or_insert_with`'s closure (e.g., due to `AtomicU64::new`
on an OOM allocator that's been replaced via a custom GlobalAlloc)
poisons the lock once and the recorder is dead until restart.

Per the project's stated cancel-correctness contract (README §Platform
Model), every component on the captured-event path should DEGRADE
under partial failure, not become un-callable.

## Suggested fix

Replace `.lock().unwrap()` with `.lock().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner())`
on every call site in this file:

```rust
fn next_pane_seq(&self, pane_id: u64) -> u64 {
    let mut map = self.pane_sequences
        .lock()
        .unwrap_or_else(|poisoned| poisoned.into_inner());
    let counter = map.entry(pane_id).or_insert_with(|| AtomicU64::new(0));
    counter.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed)
}
```

The data is just a `HashMap<u64, AtomicU64>` — there's no half-modified
state that recovery would corrupt. Recovery from poison is safe.

Bonus: per-pane sequence allocation is on a hot path. Once the entry
exists, subsequent fetches need no Mutex at all. Use `DashMap`
(already in workspace deps per the audit of redactor.rs context)
or a `RwLock<HashMap>` with the upgrade-only-on-insert pattern. That
removes the lock-poisoning risk entirely AND eliminates lock
contention under high-rate capture.

## Acceptance criteria

- All five `.lock().unwrap()` sites in `replay_capture.rs:73, 88,
  98, 104, 209` use the poison-recovery pattern.
- A regression test poisons the lock (via a panicking thread) and
  asserts subsequent calls succeed.
- Bonus (optional): perf bench shows no contention on the
  steady-state already-existing-key path.

## References

- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/replay_capture.rs:73, 88, 98, 104, 209`
- README §Replay & Forensics
- Skill: multi-pass-bug-hunting Pass 1 (lock-unwrap-on-hot-path is
  a Category 1 panic risk in the skill's classification)

 {"id":"ft-xydxz","title":"[review][LOW] ft-eze48 ftvi fuzz corpus has only one seed (FTVI magic + EOF), sparse coverage for libfuzzer starting points","description":"commit 6567397b landed fuzz/fuzz_targets/ftvi_index_from_bytes.rs with a single corpus seed at fuzz/corpus/ftvi_index_from_bytes/short_magic. Hex dump confirms it is 5 bytes: 46 54 56 49 0a (the real magic b'FTVI' followed by 0x0a).

The seed uses the correct magic bytes (verified against crates/frankenterm-core/src/search/vector_index.rs:10 const MAGIC = b'FTVI'). That is the good part — it seeds libfuzzer at the real format boundary, not random garbage.

Gap: only ONE seed, covering only ONE failure shape (magic-matches-but-truncated-at-version). A properly-seeded fuzz target typically ships 5-10 seeds exercising:
- Empty input (zero bytes)
- Minimal valid FTVI: magic + version=1 + dimension=0 + count=0 (12 bytes total, no records)
- Single-record FTVI: magic + version=1 + dimension=1 + count=1 + one (u64 id, u16 f16-value) record
- Magic-only (4 bytes — truncates before version)
- Valid magic + valid version + truncated at dimension (6 bytes)
- Valid header claiming count=1 + zero record bytes (parser must not OOB)
- Valid header + bogus version (e.g., version=255) to exercise version rejection

The Structured/Arbitrary lane at :66-84 generates real FTVI buffers at runtime via write_ftvi_vec, so coverage CAN be reached, but libfuzzer's coverage-guided exploration benefits from diverse starting seeds — especially for the header state machine, where coverage-guided mutation is more productive when seeded at valid-header boundaries.

Fix: add 5-7 curated seed files to fuzz/corpus/ftvi_index_from_bytes/ constructed via write_ftvi_vec and dd/printf. One-time scripting cost.

Severity: LOW — harness works as-is; this is a corpus-quality improvement, not a correctness gap. Audit round 14, 2026-04-23.
